title: Geothermal revolution approaches critical moment | Wood Mackenzie
gist: Fervo Energy's ~US$1.9bn Nasdaq IPO has galvanised enhanced geothermal systems (EGS), a fracking-derived path to 24/7 zero-carbon dispatchable power attractive to hyperscalers; Wood Mackenzie sees bright prospects and a rare North American cost lead over China, but EGS will meet only ~0.2% of the 2026-2035 global power-demand increase.

themes.theme: EGS as a fracking-derived energy platform

insights.claim: Fervo Energy uses horizontal drilling and hydraulic fracturing to flow hot water through low-permeability rock, making enhanced geothermal power generation possible in a far wider range of locations than conventional geothermal, which needs water naturally flowing through hot rock.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: EGS turns the US shale toolkit into a clean-power growth vector; watch oilfield-services and E&P names with EGS exposure as a non-obvious clean-energy play.
insights.quote: By using horizontal drilling and hydraulic fracturing, Fervo’s enhanced geothermal system is able to flow hot water through rocks with much lower natural permeability.
insights.locator: Conventional geothermal power systems rely on water

insights.claim: EGS builds on America's comparative advantages in horizontal drilling and hydraulic fracturing, giving the technology a structural US tailwind and bipartisan political support.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: EGS is a domestic-advantage industrial theme; US policy support is more durable than for most clean-energy categories.
insights.quote: That combination of attributes has helped build support for next-generation geothermal energy across the political spectrum in the US.
insights.locator: Fervo has a wide range of backers

insights.claim: Fervo's backers and partners include E&P company Devon Energy and fracking firm Liberty Energy, whose former CEO Chris Wright is the current US energy secretary, tying EGS directly to the oil-and-gas establishment and federal energy policy.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: The energy secretary's prior tie to Fervo's ecosystem signals favourable regulatory posture toward EGS; a policy edge worth tracking.
insights.quote: the hydraulic fracturing firm Liberty Energy, where the current US energy secretary Chris Wright was previously chief executive
insights.locator: Fervo has a wide range of backers

insights.claim: Beyond Fervo, EGS is being developed by Sage Geosystems, 400C Energy and Ormat, while Eavor pursues closed-loop advanced geothermal systems (AGS) that avoid hydraulic fracturing, and Quaise is developing millimetre-wave drilling for geothermal wells.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: A named peer set for sizing the EGS/AGS investable universe; Ormat is the listed incumbent, the rest are emerging private plays.
insights.quote: some are specialising in particular segments of the value chain, including Quaise, which has innovative drilling technology using millimetre waves to bore wells for geothermal plants

themes.theme: Hyperscaler demand for dispatchable clean power

insights.claim: Big-tech hyperscalers are key EGS buyers because their data centres require 'five nines' (99.999%) reliability while meeting emissions targets, a combination that intermittent solar and wind cannot satisfy.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Data-centre power reliability needs are a structural offtake demand source for dispatchable clean power; links AI buildout to geothermal demand.
insights.quote: they often require “five nines” reliability for their data centres, meaning that they are available 99.999% of the time
insights.locator: The big tech hyperscalers are among those buyers

insights.claim: Meta and Google have been the most active hyperscalers in EGS partnerships, with Google serving as the ultimate offtaker for Fervo's first plant, the 3.5-MW Project Red in Nevada.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Identifies anchor offtakers validating EGS commercially; Google/Meta procurement signals are leading indicators for the sector.
insights.quote: Google was the ultimate offtaker for Fervo’s first project, a small 3.5-megawatt plant in Nevada called Project Red.
insights.locator: Meta and Google have been the most active

insights.claim: Fervo's first large-scale project, Cape Station in Utah, is expected to bring its 100-MW first phase online early next year, followed by a 400-MW Phase 2 in 2028; until it has a demonstrated track record investors will see EGS execution risk.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Cape Station's early-2027 startup is the key de-risking catalyst for the EGS thesis; a concrete date to watch for Fervo and peers.
insights.quote: The 100-MW first phase is expected to come online early next year, followed by the 400-MW Phase 2 in 2028.
insights.locator: All eyes are now on Fervo’s first large-scale project

insights.claim: EGS faces social-licence risk because in some parts of the world the mention of 'fracking' alone could trigger a ban, even though EGS delivers zero-carbon power; it is most likely to scale quickly in the US where fracking is already accepted.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Geographic scaling will be highly uneven; the US is the early winner, anti-fracking jurisdictions (e.g. parts of Europe) are stranded markets.
insights.quote: In some parts of the world, the mere mention of “fracking” might be enough to build momentum for a ban on EGS, despite its advantages as a source of zero-carbon power.
insights.locator: Kate Adie, a Wood Mackenzie analyst

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie estimates that in the US, EGS plants will be significantly higher-cost than solar or wind but highly competitive against other dispatchable technologies such as gas-fired power plants.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Frame EGS economics against dispatchable peers (gas, nuclear), not against solar/wind; it competes for the firm-power slot, not the bulk-energy slot.
insights.quote: EGS plants will be significantly higher-cost than solar or wind power, but highly competitive against other dispatchable technologies such as gas-fired power plants
insights.locator: Our estimates for the levelised cost of electricity

insights.claim: In EGS, North America is currently the world's lowest-cost region thanks to its fracking and horizontal-drilling expertise, reversing China's usual cost leadership across most generation technologies; China's EGS costs are projected to remain only a little below North American levels into the 2030s.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: A rare clean-energy category where the US, not China, holds the cost lead; durable into the 2030s, supporting a domestic-supply-chain thesis.
insights.quote: In EGS, however, it is North America that is currently the lowest-cost region, because of its expertise in hydraulic fracturing and horizontal drilling.

insights.claim: Worldwide next-generation geothermal projects announced total only about 1.9 gigawatts, and Wood Mackenzie's base case projects global geothermal generation rising ~22 TWh (about 40%) between 2026 and 2035.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Sizes the near-term pipeline; 1.9 GW announced is tiny, so the sector is still a venture-stage growth story, not a base-load contributor.
insights.quote: next-generation geothermal projects have been announced with a total capacity of about 1.9 gigawatts, according to Wood Mackenzie data

insights.claim: Forecast geothermal generation growth (conventional plus EGS) meets only a little over 0.2% of the projected ~9,000 TWh increase in global power consumption from 2026 to 2035, so EGS is unlikely to materially affect immediate energy-system challenges.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Tempers the hype: EGS is a niche firm-power solution, not a demand-meeting technology this decade; size positions accordingly.
insights.quote: the forecast increase in geothermal production, which includes both conventional and EGS projects, meets only a little over 0.2% of the increase in global power consumption
insights.locator: Over the same period, annual global power demand

insights.claim: FERC has launched its plan to expedite grid connections for large loads such as data centres, issuing targeted orders to each of the six US regional grid operators to justify or reform their load-connection rules, following energy secretary Chris Wright's October directive to accelerate 'speed to power'.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Regulatory tailwind for data-centre power buildout; faster interconnection helps utilities and behind-the-meter / co-location generation plays.
insights.quote: calling on them to either justify or reform the rules they use for connecting large loads to the grid
insights.locator: FERC issues orders to expedite grid connections

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie analysis finds that even in a highly optimistic scenario where all advanced-stage projects proceed, the EU will face a CO2 storage shortfall in 2030 of at least 17.5 mtpa, about 35% of its 50-mtpa Net Zero Industry Act target.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: EU carbon-storage capacity will fall well short of mandate, implying compliance gaps and demand for CCS buildout or alternative abatement.
insights.quote: the EU will still face a storage shortfall in 2030 of at least 17.5 million tons per annum (mtpa) of carbon dioxide, about 35% of the 50-mtpa target

insights.claim: SpaceX joined Nasdaq in a record-breaking IPO raising US$75 billion and, as of the article, traded 37% above issue price for a market cap of about US$2.4 trillion, with Musk committed to developing data centres in space.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Space-based data centres are an emerging energy/compute theme; the SpaceX IPO marks growing capital flowing to off-planet infrastructure.
insights.quote: giving SpaceX a market capitalisation of about US$2.4 trillion
